infobox.key: Laurindo José de Araújo Almeida Nóbrega Neto
infobox.value: (2 September 1917 – 26 July 1995) was a Brazilian guitarist and composer in classical, jazz, and Latin music. He was one of the pioneers in the creation of [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova"). Almeida was the first guitarist to receive [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") for both classical and jazz performances. His discography encompasses more than a hundred recordings over five decades.[[2]](#cite_note-OliverM-1995-08-01-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Brazilian guitarist and composer (1917–1995)

In this [Portuguese name](/wiki/Portuguese_name "Portuguese name"), *"Neto"* is a [generational suffix](/wiki/Generational_suffix "Generational suffix") meaning "grandson", which is used for someone whose name is the same as their father and grandfather, like "III" in English.

| Laurindo Almeida | |
| --- | --- |
| [Almeida circa 1947/48](/wiki/File:Laurindo_Almeida_Gottlieb.jpg "Almeida circa 1947/48") Almeida circa 1947/48 | |
| Background information | |
| Born | Laurindo José de Araújo Almeida Nóbrega Neto[[1]](#cite_note-1)  (1917-09-02)2 September 1917  [Iguape](/wiki/Iguape "Iguape"), [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), Brazil |
| Died | 26 July 1995(1995-07-26) (aged 77)  [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), U.S. |
| Genres | [Classical](/wiki/Classical_guitar "Classical guitar"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova") |
| Occupations | Musician, composer |
| Instrument | Guitar |

Musical artist

**Laurindo José de Araújo Almeida Nóbrega Neto** (2 September 1917 – 26 July 1995) was a Brazilian guitarist and composer in classical, jazz, and Latin music. He was one of the pioneers in the creation of [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova"). Almeida was the first guitarist to receive [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") for both classical and jazz performances. His discography encompasses more than a hundred recordings over five decades.[[2]](#cite_note-OliverM-1995-08-01-2)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Laurindo José de Araújo Almeida Nobrega Neto was born in the village of Prainha, Brazil near [Santos](/wiki/Santos,_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Santos, São Paulo") in the [state of São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)").[[2]](#cite_note-OliverM-1995-08-01-2)

Born into a musical family, Almeida was a self-taught guitarist. During his teenage years, Almeida moved to São Paulo, where he worked as a radio artist, staff arranger and nightclub performer.[[3]](#cite_note-3) At the age of 19, he worked his way to Europe playing guitar in a cruise ship orchestra. In Paris, he attended a performance at the [Hot Club de France](/wiki/Hot_Club_de_France "Hot Club de France") by [Stéphane Grappelli](/wiki/St%C3%A9phane_Grappelli "Stéphane Grappelli") and famed guitarist [Django Reinhardt](/wiki/Django_Reinhardt "Django Reinhardt"), who became a lifelong artistic inspiration.[[4]](#cite_note-BrookesT-GAAL-4)

Returning to Brazil, Almeida continued composing and performing. He became known for playing both classical Spanish and popular guitar. He moved to the United States in 1947; a trip financed when one of his compositions, a song known as "Johnny Peddler" became a hit recorded by [the Andrews Sisters](/wiki/The_Andrews_Sisters "The Andrews Sisters"). In Los Angeles, Almeida immediately went to work in film studio orchestras.[[2]](#cite_note-OliverM-1995-08-01-2)

## Early career in the United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early career in the United States")]

Almeida was first introduced to the jazz public as a featured guitarist with the [Stan Kenton](/wiki/Stan_Kenton "Stan Kenton") band in the late 1940s during the height of its success. According to author Michael Sparke, Almeida and his fellow Kenton bandmember drummer [Jack Costanzo](/wiki/Jack_Costanzo "Jack Costanzo") "endowed the music of Progressive Jazz with a persuasive Latin flavor, and the music is enriched by their presence."[[5]](#cite_note-SparkeM-SKTIAO-5) Famed Kenton arranger [Pete Rugolo](/wiki/Pete_Rugolo "Pete Rugolo") composed "Lament" specifically for Almeida's cool, quiet sound,[[6]](#cite_note-TynanJ-TAofLA-6) and Almeida's own composition "Amazonia" was also featured by the Kenton orchestra. Almeida stayed with Kenton until 1952.

Almeida's recording career enjoyed auspicious early success with the 1953 recordings now called Brazilliance No. 1 and No. 2 with fellow Kenton alumnus [Bud Shank](/wiki/Bud_Shank "Bud Shank"), bassist [Harry Babasin](/wiki/Harry_Babasin "Harry Babasin"), and drummer Roy Harte on the [World Pacific](/wiki/World_Pacific "World Pacific") label (originally entitled "The Laurindo Almeida Quartet featuring Bud Shank").[[7]](#cite_note-RobertsJS-LZ-p74-7) Widely regarded as "landmark" recordings, Almeida and Shank's combination of Brazilian and jazz rhythms (which Almeida labeled "samba-jazz"-[[4]](#cite_note-BrookesT-GAAL-4)) presaged the fusion of Latin and jazz, which is quite different in bossa nova, although jazz critic Leonard Feather credited Almeida and Shank as the creators of bossa nova sound.[[2]](#cite_note-OliverM-1995-08-01-2)

Other observers note that the beat, harmonic stamp, and economy of expression were different from the bossa nova, giving Almeida and Shank's recording "...a different mood and sound...certainly valuable in its own right."[[8]](#cite_note-McGowanC-TBS-p179-8)

Almeida's classical solo recording career on [Capitol Records](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") began in 1954 with *The Guitar Music of Spain*. Almeida made a series of highly successful classical recordings produced by [Robert E. Myers](/wiki/Robert_E._Myers_(record_producer) "Robert E. Myers (record producer)").[[6]](#cite_note-TynanJ-TAofLA-6) Among Almeida's notable classical recordings is an album widely considered to be the first classical crossover album, the 1958 Grammy winner *[Duets with the Spanish Guitar](/wiki/Duets_with_the_Spanish_Guitar "Duets with the Spanish Guitar")* with mezzo-soprano [Salli Terri](/wiki/Salli_Terri "Salli Terri") and flutist Martin Ruderman. In this recording, Almeida arranges standard classical and folk repertoire through the prism of several Latin musical forms, including the modenha, charo, maracatu and boi bumba.[[8]](#cite_note-McGowanC-TBS-p179-8) The result, according to *Hi-Fi and Music Review* was "...a prize winner in my collection. Laurindo Almeida's guitar playing captures the keen poignancy and rhythmic élan of Brazilian music with superb assurance and taste ...".[[9]](#cite_note-9) The recording was nominated for two Grammy Awards and won for Best Classical Engineering for Sherwood Hall III at the first Grammy Awards ceremony. In her recent memoir *Simple Dreams*, singer [Linda Ronstadt](/wiki/Linda_Ronstadt "Linda Ronstadt") discusses *Duets With the Spanish Guitar* and notes that her aunt, the renowned Spanish singer [Luisa Espinel](/wiki/Luisa_Espinel "Luisa Espinel") was a friend of vocalist Salli Terri: "Knowing I wanted to sing, Aunt Luisa had sent me a recording, *Duets with the Spanish Guitar*, which featured guitarist Laurindo Almeida dueting alternately with flautist Martin Ruderman and soprano Salli Terri. It became one of my most cherished recordings."[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Of Almeida's five career Grammys, four were awarded in classical categories. His classical recording discography also includes the debut recordings of two major guitar works, [Heitor Villa-Lobos](/wiki/Heitor_Villa-Lobos "Heitor Villa-Lobos")' Guitar Concerto and [Radamés Gnattali](/wiki/Radam%C3%A9s_Gnattali "Radamés Gnattali")'s Concerto de Copacabana.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1964, Almeida again expanded his recording repertoire by joining forces with the [Modern Jazz Quartet](/wiki/Modern_Jazz_Quartet "Modern Jazz Quartet") on *Collaboration* ([Atlantic Records](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records")), which combined classical with jazz, called "chamber jazz." Almeida also toured with the MJQ, both in the 1960s and again in the 1990s.[[2]](#cite_note-OliverM-1995-08-01-2)

## Film and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Film and television")]

In addition to his recording achievements, Almeida continued his work with the film studios throughout his career, playing guitar, lute, mandolin and other instruments for more than 800 motion picture and television soundtracks (such as *[The High Chaparral](/wiki/The_High_Chaparral "The High Chaparral")* and "[The Gift](/wiki/The_Gift_(The_Twilight_Zone) "The Gift (The Twilight Zone)")," an episode of *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone "The Twilight Zone")*). Almeida made cameo appearances in the 1954 movie [*A Star is Born*](/wiki/A_Star_Is_Born_(1954_film) "A Star Is Born (1954 film)") and on a 1959 episode of *[Peter Gunn](/wiki/Peter_Gunn "Peter Gunn")* titled "Skin Deep". His performing credits included major motion pictures such as [*Good-bye, My Lady*](/wiki/Good-bye,_My_Lady_(film) "Good-bye, My Lady (film)") (1956), [*Funny Girl*](/wiki/Funny_Girl_(film) "Funny Girl (film)") (1968), and *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")* (1972). He composed the complete film scores for ten motion pictures and portions for hundreds of others, including [Charles and Ray Eames](/wiki/Charles_and_Ray_Eames "Charles and Ray Eames")'s 1957 film *Day of the Dead*.[[6]](#cite_note-TynanJ-TAofLA-6) He also underscored and performed for [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood")'s *[Unforgiven](/wiki/Unforgiven "Unforgiven")* (1992). His final film work was in *[The Cat in the Hat](/wiki/The_Cat_in_the_Hat_(2003_film) "The Cat in the Hat (2003 film)")* (2003) in which his whistling cover of "[The Girl from Ipanema](/wiki/The_Girl_from_Ipanema "The Girl from Ipanema")" is briefly heard. Some articles report Almeida won at least one Oscar award for film composition; however, while he was involved in films that were nominated, he did not receive an Oscar for his film work.

## Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Later career")]

In the 1970s, Almeida reunited with Bud Shank, forming [The L.A. Four](/wiki/The_L.A._Four_(band) "The L.A. Four (band)") with [Ray Brown](/wiki/Ray_Brown_(musician) "Ray Brown (musician)") and [Chuck Flores](/wiki/Chuck_Flores "Chuck Flores") (later [Shelly Manne](/wiki/Shelly_Manne "Shelly Manne") and then [Jeff Hamilton](/wiki/Jeff_Hamilton_(drummer) "Jeff Hamilton (drummer)")).[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGowanPessanha2009180-12) From 1974 to 1982, the L.A. Four toured internationally and recorded a series of albums for [Concord Jazz](/wiki/Concord_Jazz "Concord Jazz"), including *The L.A. Four Scores!*, an acclaimed live recording from the 1974 Concord Jazz Festival.[[13]](#cite_note-WeberB-NYT-13) In 1980, Almeida joined forces with Charlie Byrd on a series of highly regarded recordings, including *Latin Odyssey*, *Brazilian Soul* and *Tango*.[[7]](#cite_note-RobertsJS-LZ-p74-7) He also recorded with [Baden Powell](/wiki/Baden_Powell_de_Aquino "Baden Powell de Aquino"), [Stan Getz](/wiki/Stan_Getz "Stan Getz") and [Herbie Mann](/wiki/Herbie_Mann "Herbie Mann"), among others. His guitar trio, *Guitarjam*, with [Larry Coryell](/wiki/Larry_Coryell "Larry Coryell") and [Sharon Isbin](/wiki/Sharon_Isbin "Sharon Isbin") played Carnegie Hall in 1988. In the 1990s, Almeida toured again with the Modern Jazz Quartet. In 1992, Concord Records issued *Outra Vez*, an October 1991 live recording with bassist Bob Magnusson and drummer Jim Plank; *[JazzTimes](/wiki/JazzTimes "JazzTimes")* wrote that *Outra Vez* was "...a testament to his enduring genius as a concert guitarist, composer and arranger".[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In discussing *Outra Vez*, [John Storm Roberts](/wiki/John_Storm_Roberts "John Storm Roberts") noted "...there was nothing retro about its tour de force, a phenomenal duet of Almeida performing Beethoven's 'Moonlight Sonata' while bassist Bob Magnusson played Thelonious Monk's 'Round Midnight.'"[[7]](#cite_note-RobertsJS-LZ-p74-7)

## Awards and honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Awards and honors")]

Laurindo Almeida was nominated for sixteen Grammys and received the award five times:

* 1960 *The Spanish Guitars of Laurindo Almeida* Best Classical Performance Instrumental Soloist or Duo
* 1960 *Conversations with the Guitar* Best Classical Performance Vocal or Instrumental Chamber Music
* 1961 *Discantus* Best Contemporary Classical Composition (tied with [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky"))
* 1961 *Reverie for Spanish Guitars* Best Classical Performance Instrumental Soloist without Orchestra
* 1964 *Guitar from Ipanema* Best Instrumental Jazz Performance-Large Group

In 1992, he was honored with the Latin American & Caribbean Cultural Society Award for "his illustrious career as a performer and composer and his dedicated promotion of the music of the Americas." Shortly before his death, the Brazilian government honored Almeida, awarding him the "Comendador da [Ordem do Rio Branco](/wiki/Order_of_Rio_Branco "Order of Rio Branco")." In 2010, [*Fanfare*](/wiki/Fanfare_(magazine) "Fanfare (magazine)") inducted his 1958 *Duets with the Spanish Guitar* into its Classical Recording Hall of Fame.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Death")]

Almeida was teaching, recording and performing until the week before his death of leukemia on July 26, 1995, at age 77 in Los Angeles, California.[[2]](#cite_note-OliverM-1995-08-01-2)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Laurindo Almeida's archives are housed at the US Library of Congress and at [California State University, Northridge](/wiki/California_State_University,_Northridge "California State University, Northridge").[[16]](#cite_note-16) He composed more than 1000 separate pieces, including 200 popular songs. In 1952 Almeida formed his own publishing company, Brazilliance, which has been influential in the dissemination of Latin American music. As described by authors Chris McGowan and Ricardo Pessanha, Laurindo Almeida was "...an artist known for his harmonic mastery, subtle dynamics, rich embellishments and adept improvisatory skills in a variety of idioms".[[8]](#cite_note-McGowanC-TBS-p179-8)

In 2004 Almeida's guitar work from his version of *[The Lamp Is Low](/wiki/The_Lamp_Is_Low "The Lamp Is Low")* was [sampled](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") by the Japanese [DJ](/wiki/DJ "DJ") and [producer](/wiki/Music_producer "Music producer") [Nujabes](/wiki/Nujabes "Nujabes") for the song "Aruarian Dance" which features on the soundtrack to the [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") series *[Samurai Champloo](/wiki/Samurai_Champloo "Samurai Champloo")*.

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Discography")]

### As leader

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laurindo_Almeida&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: As leader")]

* *The Guitar Worlds of Laurindo Almeida* (Capitol, 1961)

* *It's a Bossa Nova World,* 'Laurindo Almeida All Stars,' including [Howard Roberts](/wiki/Howard_Roberts "Howard Roberts"), [Jimmy Rowles](/wiki/Jimmy_Rowles "Jimmy Rowles"), [Bob Cooper](/wiki/Bob_Cooper_(musician) "Bob Cooper (musician)"), [Victor Feldman](/wiki/Victor_Feldman "Victor Feldman") and [Shelly Manne](/wiki/Shelly_Manne "Shelly Manne") (Capitol, 1962)

* *Bach Ground Blues & Greens* (Century City, 1970)

* *Latin Guitar* (Dobre, 1976)

* *Clair de Lune* (Angel, 1980)

* *Selected Classical Works for Guitar and Flute* with [Bud Shank](/wiki/Bud_Shank "Bud Shank") (Concord, 1982)
* *Latin Odyssey* with Charlie Byrd (Concord Jazz, 1983)
* *3 Guitars 3* with [Sharon Isbin](/wiki/Sharon_Isbin "Sharon Isbin") and [Larry Coryell](/wiki/Larry_Coryell "Larry Coryell") (ProArte, 1985)
* *Tango* with Charlie Byrd (Concord, 1985)
